data.title: Procept BioRobotics down after CEO retirement
data.description:
• Shares of Procept BioRobotics (NASDAQ:PRCT) fell ~9% on Friday after the surgical robotics company announced that its CEO, Reza Zadno, would retire effective Sept. 1, and Larry Wood, a member of the board of directors, would succeed him.
  • Concurrently, the San Jose, California-based MedTech also preannounced its Q2 2025 results, indicating $79.2M in revenue, which exceeded $76.1M in FactSet consensus.
  • Wood joins Procept (NASDAQ:PRCT) on Sept. 2 from Edwards Lifesciences (EW), where he has functioned as corporate vice president and group president at the heart device maker’s Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement and Surgical Structural Heart units since 2023.
  • “On behalf of the board of directors, I want to thank Reza for his dedication to the Company and its mission over the past five years,” PRCT’s chair, Thomas Prescott, said.

data.title: Trump says no focus on Canada trade talks, tariffs likely to remain
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said Friday that trade talks with Canada are not currently a priority for his administration, suggesting he may opt to maintain existing tariffs rather than pursue a new agreement.

“We haven’t really had a lot of luck with Canada,” Trump told reporters Friday morning.

“I think Canada could be one where they’ll just pay tariffs, not really a negotiation,” he added. “We don’t have a deal with Canada. We haven’t been focused on that.”

The Canadian dollar showed little reaction to the comments, which echoed Trump’s earlier remarks on trade relations with Canada.

Earlier this month, President Trump announced a 35% tariff on Canadian imports, warning of additional hikes if Ottawa retaliates. The tariffs, outlined in a letter to Prime Minister Mark Carney and posted on Truth Social, are set to take effect on August 1.

The move was a blow to Carney’s efforts to secure a trade deal with Washington and marked an escalation from the previous 25% tariff Trump had imposed on Canada.

Here is a list of ETFs to track the broader U.S. market: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

And here are ETFs to track Canadian market: (BATS:BBCA), (NYSEARCA:EWC), (NYSEARCA:FLCA), (NYSEARCA:FXC).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: The Bancorp outlines $7 EPS run rate target by end of 2026 through fintech expansion and buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Bancorp, Inc. (TBBK) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Damian M. Kozlowski, CEO, reported "The Bancorp earned $1.27 per diluted share in the second quarter on year-over-year revenue growth of 11%, excluding fintech loan credit enhancement income with expense growth year-over-year of 11%." He emphasized fintech as the key revenue driver, stating GDV climbed 18% year-over-year, with fintech fee and related interest income growing 30%.
  • Kozlowski announced a 5-year expansion with Block to include debit and prepaid card issuance for Cash App customers, anticipating program launch as early as Q1 2026, and suggesting the relationship "will be very meaningful in the future to both GDV and fee growth."
  • A substantial increase to the share repurchase program was detailed, with $500 million authorized over 18 months starting in Q3 2025, funded by core earnings growth and refinancing of $100 million in maturing debt with $200 million of new senior unsecured debt, expecting $300 million of shares to be purchased for the remainder of 2025 and $200 million in 2026.
  • Kozlowski reaffirmed 2025 EPS guidance at $5.25 and introduced "Project 7," a plan targeting "at least a $7 earnings per share run rate by the end of '26" through fintech revenue growth, buybacks, and efficiency gains.
  • Martin F. Egan, Interim CFO, stated, "Excluding the consumer fintech loan credit enhancement income, noninterest income for the second quarter of 2025 was $40.5 million, which was 32% higher than the second quarter of 2024. Total fintech fees accounted for most of that increase."

Outlook

  • Kozlowski maintained guidance of $5.25 earnings per share for 2025, and described Project 7 as a pathway to "at least a $7 earnings per share run rate by the end of '26."
  • Management expects the expanded partnership with Block to enhance growth in GDV and fee income beginning as early as Q1 2026, with additional resource allocations and productivity enhancements, including AI, anticipated to contribute to earnings growth in late 2026.
  • No change in guidance from the previous quarter, with continued emphasis on fintech-driven growth and return of capital through buybacks.

Financial Results

  • The Bancorp reported $1.27 per diluted share for Q2 2025, up from $1.19 per diluted share in Q1 2025.
  • Fintech ecosystem growth remained strong, with GDV up 18% year-over-year and total fintech fee and related interest income up 30%.
  • Noninterest income excluding credit enhancement was $40.5 million, a 32% increase year-over-year, driven by fintech fees.
  • Loan balances grew 17% year-over-year; consumer fintech loans rose 871% year-over-year to $680.5 million and 19% over the linked quarter.
  • Noninterest expense for Q2 2025 was $57.2 million, up 11% year-over-year, including a 10% increase in salaries and benefits.
  • Net interest income was 4% higher than Q2 2024, with net interest margin at 4.44% compared to 4.07% in Q1 2025.

Q&A

  • Timothy Jeffrey Switzer, KBW, asked if the Block partnership was a new or replacement product. Kozlowski replied, "This is the entire portfolio of the card issuance for Block...the mandate is to replace that volume over time."
  • Switzer inquired about lower deposits; Kozlowski explained, "We actually took some savings deposits off balance sheet, and we also had $500 million of insurance deposits...that's running off plus the tax season."
  • Switzer questioned increased criticized loans and nonaccruals in the REBL book. Kozlowski responded, "We have a lot of visibility as to their business plans...we don't expect a big -- another spike in substandard assets."
  • Joseph Peter Yanchunis, Raymond James, probed on the Aubrey property; Kozlowski noted increased occupancy and pending disposition efforts: "There are about 20 units available to rent. So the occupancy has gone up dramatically...we're in active discussions with potential purchasers."
  • Yanchunis asked about expenses tied to the Block program. Kozlowski replied, "There will be incremental hires...a little bit extra in the third and fourth quarters ramping up."
  • An unidentified analyst queried about REBL loan maturities and capital availability. Kozlowski indicated flexibility: "If they're on their business -- with the vast majority...we're more than happy to extend those loans."
  • Questions focused on the Aubrey appraisal process, recognition of earnest money deposits, and NPAs in the SBL book, with Kozlowski providing detailed responses on accounting treatment, portfolio stability, and risk mitigation.
  • Switzer asked about capacity for new fintech partners. Kozlowski stated, "We have built an ecosystem where we could have 5x the volume that we have today."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about the durability of fintech growth, REBL portfolio risks, credit migration, and resource requirements for new partnerships, with a tone reflecting cautious optimism and a focus on risk management.
  • Management presented a confident, forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing their visibility, preparedness, and growth plans. Kozlowski stated, "We believe it's pretty clear cut that deposits should come to us without a lot of delay."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains consistent, probing on credit quality and fintech growth sustainability, while management's tone has grown more assertive, particularly concerning fintech expansion and share repurchase scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 EPS remains unchanged at $5.25, with this quarter introducing a more aggressive buyback program and the new Project 7 EPS target for 2026.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to highlight the expanded Block partnership and the launch of Project 7, signaling a bolder growth and capital return strategy versus the prior quarter's emphasis on organic fintech growth and asset sensitivity reduction.
  • Analysts' focus continues to center on fintech momentum and credit portfolio risk, but this quarter saw more detailed questions about the operational impact of new partnerships and expense management.
  • Management's confidence has increased, with more explicit forward-looking guidance and assertive statements about scalability and efficiency initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management discussed maturity and credit quality concerns in the REBL portfolio, with Kozlowski stating, "We don't expect there to be a lot of increases in substandard category."
  • The Aubrey property remains a work-in-progress, with pending resolution on both its sale and a disputed earnest money deposit, which management expects to resolve favorably.
  • Incremental expenses are anticipated as new fintech programs, particularly with Block, are implemented, though management expects productivity gains and AI adoption to mitigate long-term expense growth.
  • Analyst concerns included the trajectory of criticized loans, potential for further credit migration in the SBL book, and the operational scalability of fintech partnerships.

Final Takeaway

The Bancorp entered Q2 2025 with strong fintech-driven revenue growth, a significant expansion of its partnership with Block, and a bold increase in share repurchase authorization. EPS guidance for 2025 remains at $5.25, with the introduction of Project 7 targeting a $7 EPS run rate by the end of 2026. Management underscored confidence in operational scalability, fintech ecosystem leadership, and ongoing risk management within lending portfolios, while investors will closely watch execution on growth initiatives, resolution of legacy credit matters, and delivery of stated financial targets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chevron's return to Venezuela set to boost supplies at U.S. refineries
data.description:
The Trump administration's decision to let Chevron (NYSE:CVX) resume producing and exporting Venezuelan crude is set to increase crude supplies for U.S. refiners by more than 200K bbl/day, Bloomberg reported Friday.

While the specifics of the U.S. decision have not been disclosed, Chevron (NYSE:CVX) was allowed to drill and sell oil only to U.S. refiners before losing its license to operate in Venezuela at the end of May; at that time, Chevron produced 240K bbl/day in Venezuela, most of which it sent to fuelmakers including Valero Energy (NYSE:VLO) - easily the biggest U.S. buyer of Venezuelan oil - as well as Phillips 66 (PSX), PBF Energy (PBF), and its own refinery in Pascagoula, Mississippi.

Chevron (CVX) may be able to resume Venezuelan production with little delay because the country stocked up on diluent - a key input that helps oil flow through pipelines - in late May, allowing production to remain stable at ~1.1M bbl/day in the following months, despite sanctions.

It remains to be seen if the U.S. authorization will be extended to other drillers, including Spain's (OTCQX:REPYF) (OTCQX:REPYY) and Italy's Eni (E).

In the two months that a de facto ban on Venezuelan oil has been in place, crude kept flowing to China after being rebranded as Malaysian oil to evade sanctions; in June, while exports to the U.S. fell to zero, shipments to China soared to a five-year high of 563K bbl/day.

ETF: (CRAK)

data.title: Kinsale signals optimism in profitability and growth amid 24.7% six-month ROE and competitive E&S market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kinsale Capital Group, Inc. (KNSL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Michael Patrick Kehoe, Chairman and CEO, opened with, “In the second quarter of 2025, Kinsale's operating earnings per share increased by 27.5% and gross written premium grew by 4.9% over the second quarter of 2024.” He noted a combined ratio of 75.8% and a 6-month operating return on equity of 24.7%. Kehoe also emphasized, “Our book value per share increased by 16% since the year-end 2024.”
  • Kehoe highlighted Kinsale’s continued focus on “small E&S accounts,” underwriting discipline, technology advantage, and low-cost structure, reiterating confidence in the company’s prospects “for both profitability and growth in the years ahead in all types of market environments.”
  • The CEO detailed the renewal of the reinsurance program, stating, “the overall program was slightly more favorable for Kinsale upon renewal,” with increased retention on casualty and property treaties and a higher ceding commission.
  • Bryan Paul Petrucelli, CFO, stated, “Again, just another strong quarter with net income and net operating earnings increasing by 44.9% and 27.4%, respectively.” Petrucelli reported a 20.7% expense ratio and net investment income up 29.6% over the prior year, with the company’s float growing to $2.9 billion at June 30. He added, “Diluted operating earnings per share continues to improve and was $4.78 per share for the quarter compared to $3.75 per share for the second quarter of 2024.”
  • Brian Donald Haney, President & COO, commented, “The E&S market remains competitive, though the intensity varies by division. We're seeing robust premium growth in small business property, high-value homeowners, commercial auto, entertainment and general casualty.” Haney noted a 9% submission growth rate, and continued expansion of the product suite including a new agribusiness vertical and a new homeowners product in four states.

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit numerical forward guidance but reiterated confidence in long-term profitability and growth. Kehoe affirmed, “10% to 20% over the course of the cycle is a -- it's a good faith estimate and it's actually, I think, a conservative one.” The CEO acknowledged “near term, we've got some headwinds with competition,” especially in commercial property, but expects year-over-year comparisons to ease in the second half of the year.
  • Haney said, “Overall, we remain optimistic. Our loss results are good. Our growth prospects are good. And as the low-cost provider in our space, we have a durable competitive advantage.”

Financial Results

  • Operating earnings per share for the quarter were $4.78. Gross written premium increased by 4.9% year-over-year. The combined ratio was 75.8%, with 3.9 points from net favorable prior year loss reserve development and less than 1 point in cat losses. The expense ratio was 20.7% for the quarter. Net investment income rose 29.6% over the prior year. The company’s float increased to $2.9 billion as of June 30. Book value per share rose 16% since year-end 2024. On a divisional basis, Commercial Property premiums declined by 16.8% due to heightened competition and rate declines, while excluding this division, premium grew by 14.3%.

Q&A

  • Dan (BMO Capital Markets) asked about recalibrating growth targets given recent results below 10% to 20%. Kehoe responded, “10% to 20% over the course of the cycle is a -- it's a good faith estimate and it's actually, I think, a conservative one... near term, we've got some headwinds with competition.”
  • Pablo Singzon (JPMorgan) inquired about commercial property pricing and capital return strategy. Kehoe explained, “The Commercial Property division specifically is where we're seeing the most intense competition... we'll continue to evaluate capital return but want to maintain a healthy capital position.”
  • Michael Phillips (Oppenheimer) asked about casualty pricing and construction reserves. Haney noted, “some of the more longer tail lines, let's say, like construction or excess casualty would be at the higher end.” Haney added, “California used to be a very big state for us in construction, and we've kind of pivoted away from that.”
  • Jian Huang (Morgan Stanley) questioned growth drivers and specifically homeowners. Kehoe indicated growth is “probably driven mostly by new business,” and sees homeowners as a “growing opportunity for the company in a whole range of different states.”
  • Andrew Andersen (Jefferies) asked about OpEx ratio and ceding ratio trajectory. Petrucelli confirmed, “I think that is,” regarding the OpEx run rate. Kehoe said ceding ratio “will result in a little bit of a shift to a lower ceding ratio.”
  • Joseph Tumillo (Bank of America) discussed the buy commit ratio and MGA competition. Haney reported the ratio is “relatively stable.” Kehoe did not offer an opinion on the MGA cycle’s future.
  • Mark Hughes (Truist) queried on commercial property pricing and accident year trajectory. Haney said, “I would say that's fair,” regarding stable sequential pricing. Kehoe added, “we continue to be cautious around the long-tail casualty.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on competitive pressures, growth targets, pricing, and capital return, signaling a neutral to slightly cautious tone. Questions focused on sustainability of margins, market competition, and segment-specific performance.
  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Kehoe repeatedly stressed the company’s expense and underwriting advantages, using phrases like “we're quite optimistic, but we're also realistic that near term, we've got some headwinds with competition.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the analyst tone remained focused on competitive pressures and growth pace, while management’s tone was consistent, emphasizing conservative reserving and long-term opportunity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language and growth outlook remained broadly consistent with Q1, with ongoing reference to 10%-20% growth over the cycle and acknowledgment of short-term headwinds in commercial property.
  • The competitive environment in commercial property persisted, with both quarters noting 20% rate declines and heightened competition. Submission growth slowed to 9% from 11% in Q1, largely due to commercial property.
  • Management continued to highlight conservative reserving and a focus on underwriting profitability, as well as expanding product offerings, notably in homeowners and agribusiness.
  • Analyst focus continued to center on competition, growth rates, pricing, and capital allocation, with no significant change in tone.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited heightened competition, particularly from MGAs and front-end companies, especially in commercial property and construction. Haney warned, “Some front-end companies are posting unsustainable gross loss ratios of 100% or higher signaling capital destruction.”
  • The company emphasized conservative reserving practices, especially on long-tail casualty lines, in an inflationary environment.
  • Kehoe acknowledged that “near term, we've got some headwinds with competition.”

Final Takeaway

Kinsale Capital Group delivered strong operating performance in Q2 2025, with significant earnings and book value growth supported by disciplined underwriting, conservative reserving, and a low-cost model. While the commercial property segment continues to face competitive pressure and rate declines, other divisions are showing robust growth, and new product initiatives are underway. Management maintains confidence in achieving long-term profitability and growth, backed by an expanding product suite and a durable competitive advantage in expense and underwriting discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hilltop signals continued loan pipeline growth and targets 0% to 2% full-year average loan growth in 2025 as deposit costs decline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hilltop Holdings Inc. (HTH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jeremy Blue Ford, President, CEO & Chairman, highlighted that "Hilltop reported net income of approximately $36 million or $0.57 per diluted share" for Q2 2025, noting a return on average assets of 1% and return on average equity of 6.6%. Ford pointed out a meaningful increase in net interest margin and loan pipeline growth at PlainsCapital Bank, emphasizing continued strong customer demand in Texas.
  • Ford indicated that PrimeLending remains challenged by a competitive mortgage origination market, while HilltopSecurities benefited from a strong public finance issuance market and healthy wealth management performance. He also stated, "Hilltop was able to return over $46 million to stockholders through dividends and share repurchases."
  • Ford reported that PlainsCapital Bank generated $55 million of pretax income on $12.7 billion of average assets, with a return on average assets of 1.35%. He added that core deposits within Hilltop’s markets ended the period approximately $275 million higher year-over-year.
  • Ford referenced a $7.3 million credit loss reversal, driven by improved asset quality and changes in CECL modeling assumptions. He also noted PrimeLending recorded a $3 million pretax gain in Q2, including a $9.5 million nonrecurring legal settlement.
  • Ford described a 36% year-over-year increase in public finance services net revenues at HilltopSecurities, while pointing to a decline in structured finance revenues and a 43% linked-quarter increase in fixed income revenues.
  • Ford stated, "our tangible book value per share increased over the prior quarter's level by $0.54 to $30.56."
  • William B. Furr, EVP & CFO, stated, "for the second quarter of 2025, Hilltop reported consolidated income attributable to common stockholders of $36.1 million, equating to $0.57 per diluted share."
  • Furr explained that net interest income increased by 7% year-over-year, with noninterest revenues benefiting from the $9.5 million legal recovery, while expenses increased modestly.

Outlook

  • Furr outlined that Hilltop’s current rate outlook includes two rate reductions, one in the third quarter and one in the fourth quarter. He said, "we expect that NIM levels will moderate at current levels and that net interest income will likely stabilize at a few million dollars per quarter lower than what we recorded during the second quarter."
  • Furr stated, "we are adjusting our expected full year average loan growth rate to 0% to 2% for 2025."
  • Furr highlighted expectations that deposit balances will begin growing again in the second half of the year.

Financial Results

  • Hilltop reported consolidated income attributable to common stockholders of $36.1 million, or $0.57 per diluted share, as stated by CFO Furr.
  • Net interest income for the quarter reached $110.7 million, including $600,000 of purchase accounting accretion. Net interest margin increased by 17 basis points versus the first quarter to 301 basis points.
  • Total noninterest income was $193 million. Mortgage revenues declined by $12 million year-over-year, while origination volumes increased by 2% and gain on sale margins remained stable at 223 basis points.
  • Noninterest expenses rose $5 million year-over-year to $261 million, largely from variable compensation at HilltopSecurities and higher software and personnel costs.
  • PlainsCapital Bank’s allowance for credit losses declined by $8.2 million to $98 million, with net charge-offs of approximately $900,000.

Q&A

  • Wood Neblett Lay, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods: Asked about the efficiency ratio in the broker-dealer segment and if the elevated level was due to revenue mix shift. Furr responded it was "largely a reflection of the makeup of the revenue of the business" and mentioned $1.5 million of additional severance costs.
  • Lay asked about the uptick in nonvariable expense guidance. Furr explained, "we're kind of continuing to see inflation in personnel expenses, health care costs and... ongoing inflation in software expense and... contract escalators."
  • Lay questioned what enabled the higher-than-expected deposit beta. Furr cited improved analytics and a more rational marketplace, stating, "our team is focused on making sure we're positioning our overall deposit base and the rates we're paying customers in concert with kind of the competitive environment."
  • Timothy Joseph Mitchell, Raymond James: Asked about loan growth with strong pipelines yet competition weighing on growth. Furr detailed, "in commercial real estate, we've seen growth... in our C&I portfolios... the auto note portfolio... has been in decline," and explained the timing for new business to impact the balance sheet.
  • Mitchell inquired on NIM guidance. Furr said, "we expect that to kind of trend a few million dollars a quarter below that on a go-forward basis," and discussed the impact of potential rate cuts on asset sensitivity.
  • Jordan Spencer Ghent, Stephens: Asked about share repurchase appetite and M&A. Ford responded, "we bought about $68 million of our stock at about tangible book value" and noted a board authorization for an additional $35 million.
  • Ghent followed up on classified loans improvement. Furr stated, "paydowns and... refinances out were the largest drivers of the reduction."
  • Ghent asked about deposit outflows. Furr explained these were seasonal and expects deposits to return in the third and fourth quarters.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin improvement, deposit cost management, and expense guidance, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking more details on cost drivers and loan growth strategies.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone in prepared remarks, and addressed analyst questions with detailed breakdowns, occasionally noting ongoing market uncertainties.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone was similar, maintaining a focus on deposit costs, margin prospects, and expense trends. Management’s tone remains steady, with emphasis on operational discipline and adapting to market changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year average loan growth was adjusted from a prior range of 0% to 3% to 0% to 2%.
  • Net income and earnings per share declined from $42 million ($0.65 per diluted share) in Q1 to $36 million ($0.57 per diluted share) in Q2.
  • Return on average assets and equity both decreased quarter-over-quarter.
  • PlainsCapital Bank’s pretax income and average asset base both declined compared to Q1.
  • Previous quarter included a one-time gain from the sale of Moser Energy Solutions, which did not recur in Q2. Q2 included a $9.5 million legal settlement benefiting PrimeLending.
  • Both quarters saw continued focus on cost control and efficiency, but Q2 reflected higher inflationary pressures in personnel and software expenses.
  • Management's tone remained disciplined and pragmatic, while analysts continued to probe on loan growth, margin sustainability, and expense trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competition in Texas banking markets as a dampening factor for near-term loan growth.
  • PrimeLending remains pressured by elevated home prices, high rates, and affordability issues despite some increase in origination volumes.
  • Volatility in interest rates and market conditions continues to impact structured finance and fixed income businesses.
  • Management noted that the allowance for credit losses could remain volatile, subject to credit mix, loan growth, migration trends, and macroeconomic shifts.
  • Expense guidance was raised due to inflation in personnel and software costs.

Final Takeaway

Hilltop Holdings’ Q2 2025 results reflect a period of disciplined capital management, ongoing margin expansion at PlainsCapital Bank, and continued strength in public finance and wealth management, while mortgage origination and structured finance segments remain challenged by competitive and market headwinds. The company signals ongoing efforts to optimize expenses, maintain deposit discipline, and focus on prudent loan growth, targeting 0% to 2% full-year average loan growth for 2025 and anticipating further deposit inflows in the second half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SouthState outlines mid-single digit loan growth outlook and 11% dividend increase while integration accelerates in Texas and Colorado
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SouthState Corporation (SSB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John C. Corbett, CEO, highlighted the closing of the Independent Financial transaction, calling it a deal projected to be 27% accretive to earnings per share. He noted, "In the first quarter, the bank's earnings accelerated just as we forecast, but loan growth stalled with all the economic uncertainty." Corbett stated that a "57% increase in loan production from around $2 billion a quarter to over $3 billion in the second quarter" drove solid loan growth, especially in Texas and Colorado, where loan production increased 35% and non-PCD loans grew by about $200 million.
  • Corbett celebrated the successful completion of the computer systems conversion in Texas and Colorado, describing it as "one of the best we've ever done," and recognized teams across operations, IT, risk, finance, and HR for their efforts.
  • He emphasized the company's scale at $66 billion in assets and its position in "the fastest-growing markets in the country." Adjusted for merger costs, SouthState's return on assets was 1.45% and return on tangible common equity was nearly 20%.
  • Corbett announced that the Board increased the dividend by 11% due to strong earnings growth and capital levels.
  • William E. Matthews, CFO, said, "We had another good quarter with PPNR of $314 million and $2.30 in EPS." He added, "Net interest income grew by $33 million over Q1, only $2 million of which was due to higher accretion." Matthews highlighted cost of deposits at $1.84, a 5 basis point improvement, and a second quarter efficiency ratio of 49.1%.

Outlook

  • Stephen Dean Young, Chief Strategy Officer, stated, "As we think about the assumptions, the main assumptions around that are the size of interest-earning assets, the rate forecast and then lastly, the loan accretion. For us, the interest-earning assets, we reiterate our guide from last quarter that we would have average earning assets to be roughly $58 billion for the full year in 2025. And then in the fourth quarter average, we'd exit somewhere in the $59 billion."
  • Young confirmed, "We'd continue to expect NIM to be between 380 and 390 for the remainder of the year and to drift higher in 2026 as the combination of the legacy SouthState loan book continues to reprice up."
  • Corbett described loan pipeline growth as robust, saying, "The pipeline, loan pipeline increased 45% in the first quarter. But then in the second quarter, the pipelines increased another 31%." He maintained a mid-single digit loan growth outlook for the next couple of quarters, with the possibility of moving to mid- to upper single digits in 2026 if the yield curve becomes more favorable.

Financial Results

  • Matthews reported, "Noninterest income of $87 million was similar to Q1 levels, with improvement in our correspondent business, offset by a slight decline in our mortgage revenue."
  • Matthews noted, "NIE of $351 million was at the low end of our guidance and our second quarter efficiency ratio of 49.1% brought the 6-month year-to-date ratio below 50%."
  • Provision expense was $7.5 million, "essentially matching our 6 basis points in net charge-offs." Matthews highlighted a "one additional day 1 PCD charge-off of $17 million on an acquired independent relationship."
  • Tangible book value per share was $51.96, up 8.5% from a year ago level, despite dilution from the IBTX merger.

Q&A

  • Catherine Fitzhugh Summerson Mealor, KBW: Asked about margin outlook and potential for further NIM expansion. Stephen Dean Young responded, "No significant change to the guidance as we see it... we'd continue to expect NIM to be between 380 and 390 for the remainder of the year."
  • John Eamon McDonald, Truist Securities: Inquired about deposit costs and outlook. Young explained, "We've optimized the deposit base... those deposit costs on an incremental basis will go up a little bit."
  • Stephen Kendall Scouten, Piper Sandler: Questioned interest rate sensitivity and M&A strategy. Young detailed, "We expect somewhere in the 1 to 2 basis point improvement to overall margin for every 25 basis point cut."
  • Jared David Wesley Shaw, Barclays: Asked about regulatory scale and readiness for higher asset thresholds. Corbett stated, "We're a long way from $100 billion of $66 billion in size. So we've got a long time to continue building the infrastructure."
  • Michael Edward Rose, Raymond James: Requested expense outlook updates. Matthews answered, "Really no change to our prior guidance... consensus, you guys have, we think, a good number in there for the remainder of this year and 2026."
  • Samuel Varga, UBS: Asked about reserve adjustments and capital allocation. Matthews said, "We did actually adjust our scenario wanting this quarter a little bit more pessimistically than it was in the first quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on margin sustainability, deposit costs, and growth trajectory, displaying a slightly positive tone as management provided detailed guidance and explicit targets.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, with Corbett describing the integration as "one of the best we've ever done" and Young reiterating guidance and offering precise details. In Q&A, management was direct, occasionally hedging with phrases like "we'll see how the growth outlook evolves over time."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts appear more confident, with less focus on uncertainty and more on the positive impact of integration and pipeline growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features a completed core systems integration for Texas and Colorado, and a spiked loan production from $2 billion to over $3 billion, while the previous quarter saw stalled loan growth and ongoing integration efforts.
  • Dividend was increased by 11% this quarter, while last quarter emphasized capital flexibility but did not announce a dividend change.
  • Both quarters maintained a margin guidance of 380 to 390 but this quarter's higher NIM of 402 and improved cost of deposits signal operational improvements.
  • Analysts' concerns shifted from merger execution and loan growth uncertainty in Q1 to a focus on NIM sustainability, deposit costs, and organic growth in Q2.
  • Management's tone evolved from cautious optimism in Q1 to greater confidence and emphasis on organic growth and capital deployment options in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management adjusted scenario weightings "a little bit more pessimistically than it was in the first quarter" regarding reserves.
  • There was a "one additional day 1 PCD charge-off of $17 million on an acquired independent relationship."
  • Deposit costs expected to rise incrementally as loan growth accelerates.
  • Regulatory changes at higher asset levels remain a distant but ongoing consideration.

Final Takeaway

SouthState Corporation completed the integration of Independent Financial and delivered a strong second quarter with significant acceleration in loan production—particularly in Texas and Colorado—leading to improved financial returns and an 11% dividend increase. The company reaffirmed its mid-single digit loan growth outlook, maintained disciplined cost control, and remains confident in its ability to drive organic growth and shareholder value as it continues to scale in some of the country’s fastest-growing markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: US stocks with the highest share trading volume as speculative trading takes center stage – GS
data.description:
As investors have been witnessing sharp increases in speculative trading, Goldman Sachs strategists gathered the 30 U.S. stocks that have seen the highest share trading volumes in the past month.

The most actively-traded stocks in the last month include most of the Magnificent 7 – aside from Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) – as well as digital asset companies and those in quantum computing.

Ben Snider, senior U.S. portfolio strategist at Goldman Sachs, noted that the median stock in this group has moved by an absolute magnitude of 28% during the past 12 months, whether up or down, with their valuation multiple increasing alongside their speculative trading activity.

“Recent U.S. equity trading volumes have increasingly centered on high-multiple stocks,” Snider said. “The median EV/sales multiple of the 50 stocks with the highest share trading volumes, a simple measure that has historically tracked closely with the Speculative Trading Indicator, has also surged during the last few months. Today, that EV/sales multiple equals 8x, the highest level in recent decades outside of 2000 and 2021.”

Here are the top Russell 3000 (NYSEARCA:IWV) stocks with the highest share trading volumes in the past month:

data.title: Stellar Bancorp signals continued loan growth momentum as originations nearly double in Q2 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stellar Bancorp, Inc. (STEL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert R. Franklin opened by highlighting that "new loan originations, nearly doubling in the second quarter when compared to the first," marked the highest level since 2022 and indicated a return to organic growth. He emphasized the opportunity created by increased M&A activity in Texas, stating this "potential for both customer acquisition and talent as a result."
  • Franklin reiterated the company's focus on relationship banking and its strategy to become "the bank of choice in our markets for small business leaders."
  • CFO Paul P. Egge reported, "second quarter 2025 net income of $26.4 million or $0.51 per diluted share, which is up from net income of $24.7 million or $0.46 per share in the first quarter." Egge pointed to non-interest expense management and low credit costs as key performance highlights, while noting that net interest income was $98.3 million due to "lower earning assets and slightly lower net interest margin for the quarter."
  • Egge also underscored the company's ongoing share repurchase activity, stating, "we bought back 791,000 shares of our stock at a weighted average price of $26.08 per share during the quarter."

Outlook

  • Management maintained a focus on delivering positive operating leverage and supporting loan growth in the second half of the year, with Franklin noting, "Our pipeline is healthy and continues to support growth."
  • Egge stated, "We continue to feel good about our ability to defend and incrementally improve our top-tier margin profile," and described a strategy of holding expenses flat unless "opportunistic" investment in talent or growth opportunities arises.
  • Vitulli indicated that the bank is "well positioned" to benefit from market disruption and competition, especially in funding and customer acquisition.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q2 2025 was $26.4 million or $0.51 per diluted share, compared to $24.7 million or $0.46 per share in Q1.
  • Net interest income was $98.3 million, a slight decrease from $99.3 million in the prior quarter, attributed to "lower earning assets and slightly lower net interest margin."
  • Net interest margin for the quarter was 4.18%, down from 4.2% in Q1. Excluding purchase accounting accretion, net interest margin was 3.95% versus 3.97% in Q1.
  • Provision for credit losses was $1.1 million, primarily reflecting an increase in allowance for unfunded commitments, with allowance for credit losses on loans ending at $83.2 million or 1.14% of loans.
  • Non-interest income rose to $5.8 million from $5.5 million in Q1, benefiting from "additional earnings from Federal Reserve Bank dividend as a result of Stellar becoming a member of the Fed at the beginning of the second quarter."
  • Non-interest expense remained essentially flat at approximately $70 million.
  • Tangible book value increased 10.8% year-over-year to $19.94 per share.

Q&A

  • David Pipkin Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about the competitive landscape for loans and timing for growth acceleration. Vitulli responded that originations "nearly doubled in the second quarter compared to the first," and the pipeline supports continued growth, with $640 million originated in Q2.
  • Feaster also queried on funding strategy and stability of funding costs. Vitulli explained, "On the money market, there's absolutely competitive part there," and described a measured approach with exception pricing where needed and a focus on expanding both existing and new customer relationships.
  • Feaster asked about expense management. Egge characterized their approach as "holding the line where we can," enabling them to be "opportunistic when the right opportunities come up." Franklin added that the back office build is "pretty well done" and the bank remains open to new talent acquisitions to support growth.
  • William Bradford Jones, KBW: Inquired about deposit cost competition and margin outlook. Egge said, "We feel really good about our ability to defend our margin" and explained that funding composition will drive margin improvements.
  • Jones asked about the bond portfolio. Egge confirmed satisfaction with the current level, focusing on loan growth while maintaining a "great liquid balance sheet."
  • Jones asked about share repurchase versus organic growth. Egge stated, "Organic growth is the #1 use of capital," with buybacks used opportunistically based on price.
  • Matthew Covington Olney, Stephens: Sought details on the mix of originations and progress on the C&I strategy. Vitulli confirmed a "good mix of C&I" and backfill in CRE and C&D, with a continued push into C&I opportunities.
  • Olney asked about expense trends. Egge said, "Absent opportunistic investment, that's the goal, is hold the line right here."
  • Olney questioned the 4% margin target and the impact of potential Fed cuts. Egge responded, "It will benefit the margin," and the goal remains to return to a "4 handle on margins."
  • John Lawrence Rodis, Janney: Asked about the other income line. Egge indicated the Fed dividend will be an ongoing benefit, though some lumpiness remains possible.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was positive, focusing on growth acceleration, margin defense, and capital strategy, while probing for clarity on competitive pressures and expense discipline.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone, with Egge stating, "We feel really good about our ability to defend our margin," and Franklin emphasizing the company's strong positioning and disciplined approach.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited increased optimism, reflecting improved loan origination trends and stable expense management.
  • Management responses during Q&A were detailed and aligned with prior strategic messaging, showing no signs of defensiveness or hesitation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter showed a shift from cautious optimism to more active growth, with new loan originations nearly doubling and management expressing confidence in sustained momentum.
  • Guidance language was more assertive regarding growth prospects, while expense management remained a consistent theme.
  • Analysts shifted focus from questions about economic uncertainty and credit risk in Q1 to growth acceleration, margin sustainability, and capital deployment in Q2.
  • Net income and earnings per share rose from Q1 to Q2, while net interest income and margin declined slightly.
  • Management's tone evolved from caution in Q1 to a more proactive and confident stance in Q2, supported by improved operational metrics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified competitive pressures in funding, particularly in money market accounts, but described a "measured approach with exception pricing where we need it."
  • Some disruption from regional M&A activity was acknowledged as both a challenge and an opportunity for customer and talent acquisition.
  • No new significant credit risk concerns were highlighted, with low net charge-offs and stable allowance for credit losses.
  • Analysts probed about the sustainability of expense control and margin improvements, but management expressed confidence in their current strategies.

Final Takeaway

Stellar Bancorp's Q2 2025 call showcased a marked return to growth, highlighted by a significant increase in loan originations and a steady focus on disciplined expense management. Management emphasized their strong core balance sheet, ongoing share repurchases, and readiness to capitalize on both organic and opportunistic growth, while maintaining a confident outlook for further momentum in the second half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Some Core Scientific holders may vote against CoreWeave deal - CNBC
data.description:
Some Core Scientifc (NASDAQ:CORZ) holders are upset about the deal with CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) and it's possible some activists start a vote down the deal campaign.

The Core Scientific (NASDAQ:CORZ) holders want to get a better ratio in the stock deal with CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV), CNBC's David Faber reported earlier, citing some unidentified Core Scientific holders.

"They're saying hey you are going to have to up that ratio otherwise we are going to vote the deal down," Faber said on the business network.

Earlier this month, CoreWeave (CRWV)  agreed to acquire data center infrastructure provider Core Scientific (CORZ) in an all-stock deal, implying a total equity value of about $9 billion at the time of the announcement. Under the merger agreement, Core Scientific stockholders will receive 0.1235 newly issued shares of CoreWeave Class A common stock for each share of Core Scientific common stock based on a fixed exchange ratio.
